planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 the ideal sowing windows are September–November for fall/winter crop and February–March for spring. Bright Lights tolerates light frost and light heat but bolts in sustained high temperatures. In zone 9, fall-sown chard is often the finest and can continue through winter.

directions: Sow 1/2 inch deep and thin to 6–9 inches; chard seed is a cluster (multigerm) so thinning is essential. Bright Lights is an All-America Selections winner rainbow chard that transformed chard's reputation from a workmanlike spinach substitute to a showstopping ornamental-edible. The plants produce stems in a vivid spectrum of yellow, orange, red, pink, purple, and white — a single bed creates extraordinary visual impact in the garden. Leaves are tender, mild, and slightly earthy, superior to red chard for flavor; stems are crisp and colorful even when cooked, with a mildly sweet, earthy flavor that stands up to sautéing, braising, and pasta dishes. For zone 9 fall gardens, Bright Lights is one of the most reliable cool-season crops: it germinates quickly in September warmth, grows through October–November, survives light freezes, and can continue producing into January–February. Harvest outer leaves regularly for continuous production; plants can produce for months. Tolerates partial shade in hot weather, which extends the harvest season significantly.
sources: All-America Selections — Bright Lights Swiss Chard AAS winner 1998
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Bright Lights variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Swiss Chard for Florida Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Swiss Chard Production

planting.note: In zone 9, sow seeds or set transplants in October–November for harvest in March–April; Red Creole is a short-day onion (requires about 12–13 hours of daylight to bulb) well-matched to the Gulf South's winter day length. Direct sow or set transplants 4 inches apart.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Red Creole C-5 is the predominant short-day red onion of the Gulf South, widely grown in Louisiana, Mississippi, Texas, and Alabama and a staple of Gulf Coast kitchen gardens for generations. Unlike the mild, low-pungency sweet onions prized in other regions, Red Creole is a distinctly pungent, storage-type red onion — strongly flavored, with a sharp, assertive onion bite that makes it preferred for Creole and Cajun cooking where onion flavor needs to stand up through long cooking in jambalayas, étouffées, and red beans. The medium-sized, somewhat flattened bulbs cure well and keep for several months in a dry, well-ventilated location. Red Creole bolts in long days, so it MUST be grown on the short-day winter schedule in zone 9 (planted October–December, harvested March–April). It is heat-tolerant once past the bulbing stage and handles the warming temperatures of spring well. A foundational vegetable of Louisiana foodways alongside celery and bell pepper in the Cajun 'holy trinity.'
sources: LSU AgCenter Publication — Short-Day Onion Varieties for Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Onion Variety Selection for the Gulf South
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Red Creole Onion notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Onion Production for Florida

planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost for spring; in zone 9 sow September–November for the superior fall crop. Southern Giant Curled is cold-hardy to about 20°F when established and thrives in the cool, mild winters of the Gulf Coast.

directions: Broadcast seeds and rake in lightly, or sow 1/4 inch deep and thin to 12–15 inches; Southern Giant Curled is the classic all-purpose mustard green of the American South, grown for over a century in Gulf Coast and Southeastern gardens and still the standard by which other mustard greens are judged for traditional Southern cooking. The large, heavily ruffled and curled, bright yellow-green leaves are pungent and mustardy, with a characteristic bite that is best tamed by long, slow cooking with pork (a smoked ham hock or fatback) in the traditional Southern style. The big, broad leaves are also excellent braised in olive oil with garlic for a more Mediterranean preparation, or wilted into beans and soups. Flavor improves dramatically after a frost — the cold converts starches to sugars and mellows the pungency, making fall mustard greens distinctly better than spring. In zone 9, plants sown in October are typically at their peak in December–January. Harvest outer leaves for continuous production; entire heads can also be cut at once for batch cooking. An exceptionally easy, productive, nutritious cool-season crop.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Southern Giant Curled documentation
Seed Savers Exchange
LSU AgCenter — Mustard Greens for Louisiana Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Mustard Green Production

common_name: Spicy Globe Basil (Globe Basil, Bush Basil)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 start in January–February and transplant February–March. Spicy Globe naturally forms a mounded, spherical shape without pinching — simply harvest regularly and it maintains its globe form. Excellent container herb.

directions: Transplant into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Spicy Globe is a compact, naturally mounded basil variety that forms a near-perfect sphere shape 10–14 inches in diameter without any pruning or training. The tiny, fine-textured leaves are intensely fragrant and spicy — more intensely basil-flavored than large-leaf Genovese types — with a sweet, warm, clove-like spice note that makes Spicy Globe excellent for herbes de Provence blends, compound butters, and infused oils. The self-regulating globe shape and compact 10–12 inch height make it an ideal container plant, a neat edging for herb beds, and a productive houseplant in a sunny window. In zone 9's warm season it thrives April–October; the tiny leaves wilt at the first frost. Harvest by snipping 1/3 of the plant at a time to maintain the globe shape; never remove more than 1/3 at once. The dense, tight growth resists bolting slightly better than Genovese types, giving a longer leaf-harvest window before flowers appear.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Spicy Globe Basil variety notes
Renee's Garden Seeds
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Herb Production Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil for Florida Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow 4–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 sow September–November for fall crop. Green Wave is cold-tolerant to about 15°F when established, making it one of the most cold-hardy mustards for Gulf Coast winters.

directions: Sow 1/4 inch deep and thin to 12–18 inches; Green Wave is an AAS winner mustard green producing large, beautifully ruffled, deep green leaves with a distinctive curled and frilled edge that gives it strong ornamental value as well as excellent eating quality. Flavor is pungent and spicy like Southern Giant Curled but with finer leaf texture and more cold-hardiness. Green Wave is the recommended mustard green for raw use in salads (young leaves) and sautés where texture is prized; the intricate ruffling and curling increases leaf surface area, creating both a decorative appearance in the garden and a textural interest on the plate. In zone 9, fall-planted Green Wave produces beautifully through winter, the cold sweetening and mellowing its characteristic spicy bite — the finest mustard green eating comes after temperatures drop below 40°F. Harvest outer leaves regularly; the plant regrows vigorously. An excellent substitute for kale or collards in anyone who wants a faster-maturing, more flavorful cooking green.
sources: All-America Selections — Green Wave Mustard AAS winner
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds
LSU AgCenter — Mustard Green Production in Louisiana
